title: Universal Jaynes-Cummings Control of an Oscillator
authors: Jordan Huang, Ethan Kasaba, Thomas J. DiNapoli, Tanay Roy, Srivatsan Chakram
abstract: The Jaynes-Cummings (JC) interaction-the coherent exchange of excitations between a two-level system and a harmonic oscillator-is one of the fundamental interactions of quantum optics, realized across platforms such as cavity quantum electrodynamics, trapped ions, mechanical resonators, and superconducting circuits. Although JC interactions and qubit rotations form a universal gate set for oscillator control, practical implementations have not been demonstrated. Here we develop and experimentally demonstrate universal JC-based oscillator control by compiling arbitrary unitary gates into sequences of JC interactions and qubit rotations. In our experiment, the oscillator is realized using a mode of a high quality factor microwave cavity and the ancilla qubit using a superconducting transmon circuit, with the JC interaction implemented by a sideband interaction enabled by the Josephson nonlinearity. The native gates are constructed to be closed below a chosen cutoff photon number, encoding a qudit with suppressed leakage errors, while ancilla relaxation errors are detectable. We further find that the dispersive shift serves as a compilation resource that reduces circuit depths. We demonstrate universal qudit control and implement a single-qutrit gate set with a mean post-selected process fidelity of 96%, as well as ququart and ququint shift gates. These results establish Jaynes-Cummings control as a practical route to universal oscillator control, enabling programmable bosonic processors across a variety of quantum platforms.
summary: The paper demonstrates a method for universal control of a high-dimensional quantum system encoded in a microwave cavity. By optimizing sequences of Jaynes-Cummines interactions and qubit rotations, the researchers achieved high-fidelity control of qutrits, ququarts, and ququints. This establishes a scalable and efficient framework for implementing bosonic quantum computing.
main_problem: Achieving universal control of a high-dimensional bosonic oscillator (qudit) using the Jaynes-Cummings interaction, specifically overcoming the challenges of dispersive shifts and circuit depth scaling.
main_result: Demonstrated universal qudit control with a mean post-selected process fidelity of 96% for qutrit gates and successful implementation of ququart and ququint shift gates.
method: Decomposing arbitrary unitaries into sequences of JC interactions and qubit rotations, using gradient descent optimization for pulse parameters, and utilizing frequency chirping for error mitigation.
model_or_system: A superconducting circuit-QED platform consisting of a transmon ancilla qubit coupled to a high-quality factor microwave cavity mode, operating in a weakly dispersive regime.
key_observables: Joint qubit-oscillator Wigner tomography, qudit process tomography, and state fidelity.
important_parameters: JC interaction rate (g_JC), dispersive shift (chi_f), photon number cutoff (d), and transmon coherence times (T1, T2).
main_assumptions: Assumes a decoherence-free evolution model for some comparisons; neglects higher-order energy shifts and cavity self-Kerr; assumes the ability to stabilize temperature to prevent frequency fluctuations.
figures_summary: Energy level diagrams of the JC interaction; scaling analysis of gate infidelity and circuit depth; demonstration of qutrit/ququart/ququint shift gates via Wigner functions; and process tomography matrices for universal qutrit gates.
paper_structure: Introduction of the JC control problem; description of the circuit-QED platform and Hamiltonian; presentation of the control protocol and optimization methods; experimental demonstration of universal qutrit gates; scaling analysis for higher-dimensional qudits; and error budget/robustness analysis.
why_interesting: It provides a practical route to programmable bosonic processors by turning a known physical interaction (JC) into a universal gate set, while demonstrating how dispersive shifts can be leveraged as a computational resource.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Jaynes–Cummings control. (a) Energy level di- agram showing JC (cyan) and ancilla qubit (orange) interac- tions in a rotating frame where the qubit and oscillator have a JC detuning. The ancilla qubit is encoded in the transmon’s |g⟩and |f⟩states. The JC interaction is implemented us- ing a sideband drive detuned by δ from the |f, d −1⟩↔|g, d⟩ transition, where d−1 is a chosen photon-number cutoff. Cor- responding transitions for other photon numbers are further detuned by dispersive shifts. The pulse duration is chosen to implement a 2π rotation on the cutoff transition, closing the dynamics to the subspace within the green box. Ancilla qubit relaxation errors send the state to...

FIG. 2. Scaling analysis. (a) Gate infidelity for a shift gate as a function of circuit depth, for different qudit dimen- sions. The solid and dotted lines indicate optimization with and without JC detuning, respectively. (b) Circuit depth required to reach a target fidelity as a function of qudit di- mension, fit to a quadratic function. (c) Quadratic prefactor of the shift gate circuit depth as a function of target infidelity, showing logarithmic scaling and a reduction with detuning op- timization. (d) For Haar random unitaries, the circuit depth required to reach a target fidelity as a function of qudit dimen- sion displays a quadratic scaling. (e) Circuit depth required to reach > 99%...

FIG. 3. Qutrit shift gate. (a) Illustration of a qutrit shift gate in an oscillator. Experimentally reconstructed Wigner functions are shown for six input states (top row) and the corresponding output states after the shift gate (bottom row). (b) Sequence used for joint qubit-oscillator tomography. The oscillator state is measured using Wigner tomography after postselecting on four qubit basis states. (c) Angles of the ancilla qubit and JC rotations, and JC detunings, for a qutrit shift gate. (d) Pulse sequence corresponding to the rotation angles in (c). (e) Populations P(|g, n⟩) and P(|f, n⟩) as a function of circuit layer, measured using joint qubit–oscillator tomography for a qutrit...

FIG. 4. Process tomography of universal qutrit gates. Absolute value of the process matrix for a qutrit (a) shift gate X, (b) X2 gate, (c) Hadamard gate, (d) phase gate S, (e) T gate, and (f) π/2 Givens rotation in the |0⟩-|1⟩subspace. The solid black lines indicate the ideal gate. The full process matrices are shown in Figs. S9 and S10 of the SI. (g) Process fidelities of the universal qutrit gates after post-selection are shown as solid blue markers. Grey markers indicate results of Lindblad master-equation simulations.
